title: Securing America’s Near Abroad: Recalibrating U.S. Policy Toward Haiti
gist: Haiti’s gangs control ~90 percent of Port-au-Prince and have displaced 1.4 million people; the paper argues that successive UN-backed foreign security missions (MSS, now the Gang Suppression Force) will fail to produce lasting stability unless the U.S. pivots from “foreign boots on the ground” to rebuilding Haitian state capacity, interdicting U.S.-sourced weapons (~80 percent of guns recovered in Haiti), and cutting gangs’ elite financing.

insights.claim: Gangs control roughly 90 percent of Port-au-Prince, and the Viv Ansanm coalition alone may number 12,000–20,000 members (3,000 heavily armed), matching or outnumbering the roughly 12,000 Haitian National Police officers policing the entire nation.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: The security-force-to-gang firepower asymmetry is the core reason external missions keep failing—baseline for any Haiti risk assessment.

insights.claim: As of 2025 an estimated 1.4 million Haitians—10 percent of the population—were internally displaced, a 36 percent year-on-year increase, with violence spreading beyond Port-au-Prince into the center department and as far north as Cap-Haïtien.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: Displacement is accelerating and geographically widening—leading indicator of deeper collapse and outward migration pressure on the U.S.
insights.locator: At the same time, displacement driven by violence

insights.claim: Haiti now has no sitting elected officials after the Transitional Presidential Council was dissolved on February 7, 2026 and power transferred to Prime Minister Alix Didier Fils-Aimé, who is tasked with steering toward elections tentatively set for August 2026 amid dire security conditions.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: The political vacuum and fragile election timeline make governance continuity a key uncertainty for regional stability.
insights.locator: Today, Haiti has no sitting elected officials.

insights.claim: Haitian gangs are increasingly adopting populist rhetoric—framing themselves as protectors of the masses against corrupt elites—especially after the state introduced kamikaze drones into gang-controlled territory, turning the conflict into a battle for popular legitimacy.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: Gangs are shifting from criminal to quasi-political actors—raises the risk that heavy-handed force backfires and entrenches them.
insights.locator: Despite these efforts, the Haitian state now finds itself

insights.claim: The Kenya-led Multinational Security Support Mission never reached its authorized 2,500 personnel (peaking near 1,000), required $600 million annually to be fully operational but received only $113 million, and failed to decisively weaken gangs before Kenyan police departed in April 2026.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: Chronic underfunding is the structural failure mode—commitments and pledges consistently exceed delivery in Haiti missions.
insights.locator: In practice, the MSS encountered severe structural

insights.claim: The successor Gang Suppression Force, approved by the UN Security Council in September 2025 for a twelve-month period, expands the mission to ~5,500 personnel with police and military units and greater operational autonomy to conduct offensive operations without the HNP, but risks replicating the MSS’s shortcomings absent timely deployment and sustained funding.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: The GSF’s success is not assured; watch funding reliability and deployment speed as the failure signals repeat.
insights.locator: The Trump administration has largely built upon

insights.claim: Small-footprint interventions restore order only when paired with political stability, popular buy-in, and capable domestic forces—all three of which are absent in Haiti, which is why the MSS fell short and the 2004–2018 UN Stabilization Mission’s gains reversed once it ended.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: Predictive framework: without domestic capacity, external force yields only temporary order—recurring intervention is the default absent a strategy shift.
insights.locator: There is a substantial body of scholarship

insights.claim: A new UN Support Office in Haiti (April 1, 2026) shifts logistical funding to assessed UN contributions to fix the MSS’s voluntary-funding problem, but assessed UN funding has itself proven unreliable amid liquidity constraints, undermining the model’s sustainability.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: The funding fix may not hold—UN liquidity is itself a risk to the GSF’s durability.
insights.locator: A key departure from the MSS framework

insights.claim: Roughly 10 percent of cocaine entering the United States passes through Haiti, and weakening state capacity is increasing its attractiveness as a South American drug launch point just as routes through Mexico and the Pacific are heavily contested.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: A Haitian collapse multiplies narcotics vectors into the U.S.—directly tied to U.S. counternarcotics and border politics.

insights.claim: Located just 700 nautical miles from Florida, Haitian instability transmits quickly to the U.S. via transnational crime, irregular migration, and Caribbean contagion, with weapons flowing into Haiti also arming networks in Jamaica, the Bahamas, and Turks and Caicos through guns-for-drugs exchanges.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: Haiti is a regional arms hub whose collapse spreads instability across the Caribbean—broader than a single-country risk.
insights.locator: Third, instability in Haiti carries contagion effects

insights.claim: Roughly 330,735 Haitian nationals reside in the U.S. under Temporary Protected Status, and former DHS Secretary Kristi Noem moved to end it on February 3, 2026 (stayed by a judge), a move that risks returning over a quarter-million people to a country unable to absorb them.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: TPS termination is a live policy risk that could sharply raise migration flows and humanitarian strain.
insights.locator: While recent U.S. policies have sought to channel

themes.theme: Policy pivot: capacity, weapons, and elite financing

insights.claim: An estimated 80 percent of firearms recovered in Haiti originate in the United States, so dismantling U.S.-based gun-smuggling networks would strike at the material foundation of gang power—but U.S. gun-policy politics make supply-side regulation difficult, pushing focus toward Haitian border and maritime interdiction.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: The weapons pipeline is the addressable root cause; domestic U.S. political constraints shape which levers actually get pulled.
insights.quote: an estimated 80 percent of firearms recovered in Haiti
insights.locator: Further U.S. efforts to dismantle gun-smuggling networks

insights.claim: The author argues external security deployments cannot produce lasting stability without a parallel effort to rebuild Haitian state capacity and cut gangs off from material and financial resources, advocating a paradigm shift from foreign boots on the ground to state-capacity building.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: The central policy call—signals where U.S. funding and diplomatic effort should be redirected if Washington adopts it.
insights.quote: While external security deployments may temporarily blunt violence, they are unlikely to produce lasting stability absent a parallel effort to rebuild Haitian state capacity.
insights.locator: This paper draws on field research in Haiti

insights.claim: The Haitian state is bringing in unconventional tools—launching the P4000 program to train 4,000 new police and contracting Erik Prince’s Vectus Global for weaponized drone strikes that have killed at least 60 non-gang civilians (including 17 children) since March 2025.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: Private military contractors and drone warfare are now features of the Haiti theater—civilian-casualty backlash is a material risk to mission legitimacy.
insights.locator: In a desperate attempt to assist the beleaguered Haitian state

insights.claim: The U.S. is escalating financial pressure on gang-linked elites—sanctioning former president Michel Martelly (2024), arresting businessmen Dimitri Vorbe and Pierre Reginald Boulos (2025), and passing the House’s Haiti Criminal Collusion Transparency Act of 2025 mandating investigation and sanctions of colluding elites.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: Financial-network targeting of elites, many U.S.-resident, is the emerging third pillar of U.S. Haiti policy to watch.
insights.locator: The Trump administration has also adopted a more muscular approach

insights.claim: Haiti retains functioning if overstretched security institutions—distinguishing it from other collapse cases—but building it toward the UN-recommended minimum of 25,000 police officers requires consolidating fragmented overseas training into one or two host nations to fix doctrine and cohesion problems.
insights.speaker: Christopher Shell
insights.so_what: The narrow foundation for a durable exit exists; capacity-building specifics define whether the intervention cycle can be broken.
insights.locator: Yet a critical and often overlooked reality
